column121: We were descending on the JANNY5 Arrival.  At the KOPLE waypoint; we were given 'Direct JANNY at 8;000' by ATC.  We began our descent with Joshua Approach.  Around the JANNY waypoint; we were transferred to SoCal Approach for terminal instructions to Burbank airport. The controller initially gave us a 'descend 7;000' instruction which was followed shortly thereafter with a 'descend 5;000' this descent was well prior to the usual instructions given to Aircraft X from this location.  The crew complied with all instructions from ATC.  We were receiving vectors to intercept the ILS-Z Runway 8 Approach into Burbank.  We became aware that the controller was vectoring us to intercept the localizer between the SILEX and BUDDE intersection.  While approaching from the north at 5;000 ft MSL; we were heading over one of the small mountains northwest of Burbank Airport.  We were vectored near the highest point on that hill which is charted at 3;734 ft.  While going over the hill the aircraft gave us a 'TERRAIN; TERRAIN; PULL UP.' I looked at the RA as the first 'TERRAIN' was enunciated; and saw the RA indicator show around 1;500 feet; then jumped immediately to 2;500 feet.  The warning was only given once by the aircraft.  The PF (Pilot Flying); Captain; reported not seeing any altitude indication on the RA.  We continued the approach and the remainder of the flight was uneventful.  We aren't sure whether it was an erroneous aircraft indication or if ATC may have descended us a little too low for that sector. Clarify with ATC on altitude assigned and whether assigned altitude is appropriate for sector.

column121: Aircraft X was on ILS approach to Runway 4 at GSP when a VFR aircraft inbound to GMU went across final under Aircraft X. Traffic was called. GMU [Tower] was called. Aircraft X was below the clouds and stated he would execute missed approach if there was a RA. The pilot seemed to stay above glide slope to avoid the RA. Both aircraft eventually got each other in sight and Aircraft X landed.This is a known safety problem. The closest proximity of 2 aircraft in this situation is zero lateral and less than 100 feet vertical if the GSP arrival is on the glideslope and the VFR is in the GMU Delta airspace. However; it should not be taken for granted that aircraft on instrument and visual approaches will be at or above glideslope altitude. Since the VFR is under the GSP arrival; which is usually a jet; there are also wake turbulence issues.The Charlie and Delta airspace design is flawed. The FAA is either unwilling or unable to redesign the airspace to provide for the safety of the flying public.From my experience; the VFR aircraft involved are always inbound to GMU from SC72 (Chandelle). It is probably less than half a dozen aircraft that occasionally do this. When they depart GMU; they are instructed to fly over VPLOW when GSP Runway 4 is in use. If they were instructed to fly over VPLOW when inbound to GMU; the problem would be resolved. GMU Tower can instruct aircraft to remain outside the Delta and instruct aircraft where to enter the Delta; so there is no reason this solution would not work. It could be agreed to; briefed and implemented in 24 hours.This situation develops quickly and there are no guarantees that the controllers will see it before the RA or collision alert. When it is seen; or when the alarms go off; what does FAA management want the controllers to do?  Should we be breaking off the approach as a preventive measure?

column121: We were assigned the RNAV Rwy 35; and given direct MEYRS.  We briefed the approach and confirmed altitudes in the FMS.  ATC gave us the clearance 'Cross MEYRS at or above 14;000 [feet]; cleared for the RNAV 35.'  I was the Pilot Monitoring; and I dialed 14;000 into to the autopilot pre-select.  The Pilot Flying entered 14000 in the FMS at MEYRS and executed a direct VNAV descent to MEYRS.  This caused the altitude to turn yellow in the FMS because the next altitude on the approach; at YARUB; was 14;800 [feet].  We discussed this for a moment and I deleted the 14;800 from the FMS.  The Pilot Flying then stated; and I agreed; that we should follow the charted altitudes and we entered 15;000 for MEYRS so that we would be above the published MEA of 14800 between MEYRS and YARUB.  With my intention being to put the YARUB altitude back into the FMS; I referenced the chart and then entered 12000 for YURUB (which was an error).  After crossing MEYRS at 15;000 [feet] we began a slow VNAV descent for 12;000 [feet]; but we should have been level at 14;800 [feet] all the way to YARUB.  While cross checking our position between the map display and the Jepp chart; I began to suspect that we had made an error.  Passing about 13;000 [feet]; I asked the Pilot Flying to level off; which he did; and then I stated that we were not at the correct altitude and that we should climb.  At that moment we received a GPWS caution that gave us a one time audible alert; and the Pilot Flying began a climb.  Shortly afterwards we passed the YARUB fix; and the MEA was now 12;000 so we continued the descent.While monitoring our progress on the approach; I noticed that our altitude was below the charted MEA.  I was confused for a minute while I tried to determine if we had made an error and then realized that I had deleted an altitude; and then re-enter it; but had in fact programmed the incorrect altitude.  Looking back I can't quite explain my thought process; and after the fact my mistake seems very obvious and amateur.  I did reference the chart for the YARUB altitude; and looking at it again '12;000' is written very close to YARUB; and that's my only explanation for how I matched that altitude to that fix; but of course 12;000 is the altitude after YARUB.  While I was monitoring our progress I remember thinking that something didn't seem quite right; and it took me a minute to piece together the ATC clearance; our ensuing actions; and to come to the conclusion that I had made a mistake.  The ATC clearance to an altitude that was already below a charted MEA caused a moment of confusion and second guessing.  I removed an altitude from the FMS that was auto loaded with the approach.  A few minutes later; when I realized I had made an error; I attempted to correct it but I put the wrong altitude back in the FMS after incorrectly reading the chart.  I asked the Pilot Flying to level off while I cross checked the chart and; FMS; and our position.  I then stated that we should climb; which we did.I should not have been so quick to delete an altitude from the FMS which was loaded by selecting the approach.  Also; when entering an altitude into the FMS I need to be more careful while referencing the chart and also confirm with the Pilot Flying that I am entering the correct altitude.
column123: While descending into MJT we were cleared by ATC to pass over MEYRS at or above 14;000 feet and cleared the RNAV 35 approach. We had already briefed the approach and checked the altitudes in the FMS. The first segment between MEYRS and the next point on the approach YARUB is 14;800 feet. Just before reaching MEYRS we decided to stop our descent to pass over MEYRS at 14;000 [feet]and hold at 15;000 [feet]. After YARUB the next step down is 12;000 feet. We had been descending in VNAV to make the cross at Myers. My First Officer put 12;000 feet at YARUB and I confirmed it. In VNAV we then started a slow descent to pass over YARUB at 12;000 feet. This was a mistake on my part. We realized at about 12;700 we where wrong and below a published minimum. I stopped the descent and started a climb. We did get a momentary GPWS terrain and responded according. We climbed about 20 feet and it was gone. We had already stopped descending when we got the GPWS. Realizing we where doing the wrong thing by descending in VNAV to cross YARUB at 12;000 [feet].Allowing the Pilot Monitoring to put 12000 feet n the FMS at YARUB. Immediate once we realized our mistake.. Autopilot off. Start a climb. We never did get to 14;800 [feet] because of how we where close to YARUB. I realized that we where low but also safe and never in any danger. I feel like I should have queried ATC about the descent clearance. I should have been more vigilant with the cross at YARUB.

column121: Aircraft X was IFR inbound to CNO on RNAV RWY 26R. At about 6 miles out when he contacted me; there was traffic at his 1 o'clock and 1-2 miles same altitude Aircraft Y. Aircraft Y was not on my frequency and was just outside the Delta airspace just about 5 miles east. I gave numerous traffic calls for Aircraft Y. At about a mile and half separation; I issued the IFR to descend which led him to continue on the approach with an early descent. Aircraft Y passed behind and about 400 higher than Aircraft X. Just as Aircraft Y passed behind Aircraft X; said he had the traffic in sight and said that was scary. due to the pilot saying it was scary an NMAC would be done. Since I wasn't talking to the transitioning aircraft who was last talked to by ONT with just one traffic call then a frequency change; I was never talking to Aircraft Y.Extend the Class Delta more east so aircraft would be forced to contact CNO. have better traffic calls for aircraft from previous facility to eliminate NMAC or having ONT give the aircraft a frequency change to CNO Tower to have better control and communication with all aircraft.

column121: The Satellite position had just been combined with Departure during a relatively slow traffic period. Shortly after assuming control of their traffic an aircraft went NORDO on a heading for an approach. While trying to reestablish communications with the aircraft; Aircraft X was on a vector for the ILS approach into a different airport. The data tag had already been accepted by Tower; so when the new controller assumed the traffic; the tag had already been turned a separate color than usual. Aircraft X was given a descent to 2;100 feet by the previous controller. During the attempts to reestablish communication with the NORDO aircraft; Aircraft X ended up skirting the boundary of a 2;600 foot Minimum Vectoring Altitude at 2;100 feet before making the turn back for the approach. No other aircraft were affected. After an aircraft data tag is highlighted green; there should be a way to turn the color of the aircraft back to white.

column121: Weather was IFR at the airport. A pop up IFR aircraft who was hard to understand and follow was asking for an approach into the airport because of the weather. Aircraft X departed on an 185 heading climbing to 6;000 feet. He was issued climbing instructions to 16;000 feet and a 360 heading. Normally; he would have turned quicker but I believe because of weather the pilot turned slower. A 330 heading was issued to turn him sharper and I advised the pilot to increase rate of climb for minimum altitudes. He complied. The aircraft entered the 11;000 foot minimum IFR altitud (MIA) just above 10;000 feet. There were no issues after climbing above the MIA. With the weather in the area I should have taken into account possible abnormal aircraft characteristics. Next time I will keep in mind the factors that may hinder an aircraft to turn or climb/descend at a slower rate.
column123: I was training on two sectors combined. Weather was bad and the field was IFR. A VFR aircraft to the south of the field picked up IFR because he couldn't land otherwise. Tower called for a departure; but the IFR pick up was still in the way at 4;000 feet. Once they were clear; I released the departure on an 185 heading instead of on the SID.  Aircraft X departed; and I climbed them to 16000 feet. When they were out of 6800 feet; I turned them left heading 360. There were about 5 miles of space to the left; before the aircraft entered a higher Minimum Vectoring Altitude (MVA). I turned the plane further left to a heading of 330; but it didn't help. They ended up heading east for about 7 miles; and into an 11;000 foot MVA at 10000. My instructor stepped in and told the aircraft to expedite their climb for terrain. I should've let the aircraft climb higher prior to allowing the turn. Or; I should have let them know that I needed a tight turn.

column121: There was a recent change with how our the Military Operating Area (MOA) has been handled in conjunction with ZZZ1. For the years past the MOA has been handled as an autonomous airspace which Center cleared [aircraft] in and out of. The decision has been made to have the ZZZ1 control the aircraft in and out of this airspace by designating a new control area. This week was the first time [new control area] has been in operation since its development and we expected issues; and encountered numerous airspace violations and automation issues throughout the day. The event I am reporting was only one of the numerous issues I encountered during this 1.5 hour session; and there will be numerous reports regarding this airspace from other controllers.The situation that caused the most alarm was [Aircraft X; Aircraft Y] which is a flight of 2. We received a call from [new control area] giving us the 5 minute notice that [Aircraft X; Aircraft Y] is ready to leave the airspace. This is the procedure that is published in the Letter of Agreement and following this call [new control area] will initiate an automated radar hand-off. For [Aircraft X; Aircraft Y]; there was no hand-off initiated and [Aircraft X; Aircraft Y] called Center stating they were at 25;000 ft. descending to 13;000 ft. I look in the airspace and search the 5 limited targets and do not see [Aircraft X; Aircraft Y]'s beacon code. I then notice a beacon code 5 to 10 miles outside the airspace at 25;000 ft. which is heading back to ZZZ1 and towards some ZZZ arrivals. I quickly pair the data blocks and establish communication with [Aircraft X; Aircraft Y]. Thankfully this time the traffic into ZZZ was far enough away that this was not an immediate issue. This area [Aircraft X; Aircraft Y] was in is a very heavy area for military traffic; ZZZ arrivals; ZZZ Departures; ZZZZ1 Arrivals; ZZZZ2 Departures; and ZZZZ3 Departures. If this issue happens again during a busier session this could very well end in a TCAS/RA event or even worse due to the speed of the exiting [aircraft] speeds. Either better training on the procedures for the ZZZ1 Controllers and the [aircraft] fighter squadron; or amendments to the [new control area] procedures.

column121: I sat down next to the controller working SDWR (west) radar to assist them with a few coordinations. The sector wasn't busy but coordinations with approach (for handoffs) and ATCT (for opposite direction arrivals) would have added to the complexity. I noticed that an IFR Aircraft X talking to SEE ATCT was turning away from the airport and the final approach course northeast to a higher MVA. The aircrafts lowest indicated altitude was 2;400 ft. and the MVA in the area is 4;200 ft. and 3;300ft. I quickly called SEE to see if the pilot was okay. They responded that she was having issues and they were correcting her. The weather was broken at 3;300 ft. with few clouds at 2;400ft. The tower called me back asking if we could take the aircraft back. I asked if the aircraft was VFR or IFR. They stated IFR and I had them issue her a 270 heading and a climb to 6;000 ft. By the time the tower switched her back to us; the aircraft was westbound safely above the MVA.Since she needed special handling; I asked SDMR (Miramar) radar to work the aircraft back in and she landed safely. I truthfully never considered this to be a pilot deviation. And since the tower was talking to her at the low altitude; I knew they were handling the low altitude alert; getting her back on course and trying to see if she could cancel IFR. Only when the tower advised me she was IFR and needed to come back to us; did I consider her a special priority case. But also with my years of experience; I assumed she was a low hour IFR pilot that needed special handling.I didn't tell the OM immediately about the event because she was with the tower when she was at a low altitude and I knew they were handling the situation and would be filling out the appropriate paperwork on their end. Only a little bit later did I think; I should probably have the OM watch the replay and if anything; it may be declared an priority handling. The OM advised me it should have been a pilot deviation; but I still don't understand why a disoriented pilot would get a brasher. In the future; I won't assume the classification of an event; I'll ask the OM for their guidance.

column121: [Aircraft X] was being vectored for an ILS approach into BFI RWY (Runway) 14R by the Arrival West (1W); Arrival East (1E); Boeing Arrival (1D); and Paine (1P) sectors combined. As [Aircraft X] was being vectored traffic was issued for two VFR aircraft on 1200 codes transitioning both north and south through the localizer for Runway 14R. Due to this traffic I left [Aircraft X] at 3;000 ft. and obtained a point-out with SEA Final (1F). Despite this altitude; [Aircraft X] responded to a TCAS RA and; in an effort to protect the sea final approach course; I turned [Aircraft X] right to a heading 220. As [Aircraft X] turned southbound I observed a C172; [Aircraft Y]; being vectored for the ILS Runway 20 approach into PWT. I turned [Aircraft X] further right to a northwest heading; and measured the distance between [Aircraft X] and [Aircraft Y]. I observed approximately 3.3 miles between the two and since I had already issued the turn; I judged that sufficient separation and attempted to continue working to not fall further behind. [Aircraft X] turned slower than anticipated and caused a separation error between themselves and [Aircraft Y]. The root of this issue is the continual constant conflict between IFR jets into BFI and VFR aircraft transitioning through the localizer in both north and south flow. It is a well-known; persistent issue that has been thoroughly reported for several years. Several solutions have been proposed; but none have been adapted.The only fix for this issue is a restructuring of the Seattle Class B airspace to protect the finals in both directions at BFI. I have personally submitted redesigns for this issue that both protect BFI; and in doing so protect SEA; and allow VFR operations in and out of BFI; and are not unduly hindering to the general aviation community. If safety is truly the priority of the FAA and the Seattle region; changes will be initiated immediately. I understand the process for airspace changes take time; however; the changes will never take place if the process is not started. When the inevitable aircraft incident finally occurs; I hope that no one dies and I am not on position.
column123: [Aircraft Y] was on vector; at 2;500 ft.; to ILS Runway 20 approach at PWT. [Aircraft X]; controlled by W Sector; was on final to Runway 14R at BFI and experienced a TCAS RA with an uncontrolled VFR aircraft. The W Controller was already at a high level of complexity and volume as sectors were combined. The BFI Runway 14R final is in close proximity to PWT 20 mile final and is also underneath the Runway 16 finals at SEA. With limited options [Aircraft X] was turned to the southwest towards [Aircraft Y] with only 500 ft. between them. There are many factors that lead to this situation. These include airport proximities; sector overload; traffic complexity. There is also the issue of lack of controlled and protected airspace in and out of BFI. This is an issue controllers have been bringing to local management for years. The workforce is metaphorically beating our heads against a wall as we are asking for help and are given zero assistance in return. The final to BFI is underneath the SEA finals so we are forced to descend large; fast turbojet aircraft out of Class Bravo airspace into a very high VFR traffic area. This leads numerous TCAS situations. Many suggestions have been brought up through established reporting procedures so I will not waste my time typing them out again. The feeling of a lack of support for change is disheartening. There needs to be change made into airspace and procedures for aircraft landing at BFI; and this should be done for both arrival directions at BFI.
column125: Two S46 Approach controllers reported an aircraft on an ILS Approach to BFI had to be vectored away from two conflicting VFR aircraft not in communication with ATC which resulted with the BFI aircraft losing separation with a fourth aircraft on an ILS approach to another airport. Both reporters emphasized these situations are a recurring issue due to the insufficient Class B protected airspace in the SEA/S46 airspace.

column121: I released Aircraft X for departure from BFI in a south flow. Aircraft X reported climbing to 2;000 ft. on the NIRVANA1 SID. I radar identified Aircraft X and climbed them to 6;000 ft.. As Aircraft X was climbing through 1;800 ft. I noticed that Aircraft X appeared to be making a right turn towards the SEA VOR and not following the NIRVANA SID. I instructed Aircraft X to 'turn left immediately; left turn heading 130.' At this point; Aircraft X was starting to converge with Aircraft Y who was on final approach into SEA descending through 1;100 ft. on the ILS Approach to Runway 16R. Aircraft X was about 1.5 miles laterally separated and 700 ft. vertically separated from Aircraft Y and converging. I observed Aircraft X achieve vertical separation from Aircraft Y; then saw the turn to heading 130 start occurring. I gave Aircraft X the Brasher statement. The pilot of Aircraft X stated that it 'looks like the activation for the autopilot for the NIRVANA1 did not take place.'I honestly don't know how to fix this problem; but it is a recurring; systemic issue. This is not my first event with almost identical circumstances; and many of my co-workers have had very similar events. Attempts have already been made to alter the SID; or issue an initial heading for aircraft departing BFI; but these events still occur. The typical issue is that pilots are filing SEA.J5 and though BFI is issuing the NIRVANA1 SID and a Preferred Departure Route that would join J5 from TAOMA or SUMMA; pilot error including unchecked autopilot actions that result in a turn to SEA VOR will immediately cause convergence at the same altitude as aircraft inbound to SEA within the final approach fix; as well as any aircraft departing SEA during that time. There is very little and very subtle warning; and immediate action is necessary to recover separation and end the rapidly worsening danger of the situation. I believe in most cases it is a result of an individualized pilot deviation; and I don't know how to end those completely. The only other solution I can think of would be to completely remove/decommission the SEA VOR.

column121: Aircraft X was on an ILS Approach to Runway 16L. A VFR aircraft was skirting around AFW's Class D airspace; not in communication with AFW or D10. The VFR aircraft was eastbound then turned north and crossed over WIGZU the FAF for ILS RWY 16L at 2;600 FT MSL. Aircraft X was at the same altitude. The Local Controller called the traffic and Aircraft X reported the traffic in sight. Aircraft X reported that they had to turn off the ILS then turn back once they were clear of the traffic. The two aircraft were less than 1/2 mile apart at the same altitude head on to each other. This area is dangerous and this continues to be an issue. The airspace design is outdated. AFW airport has grown and is continuing to grow. The facility has recently been upgraded from a Level 5 Tower to a Level 6 Tower. In the last year three new air carriers have all started operating at AFW.  A contractor for the US military has relocated their maintenance base to AFW. Aerospace company X has also begun construction at their new facility here as well. Both runways were extended from 9;000 feet to 11;000 feet in the last few years. Everything at this airport has been growing and expanding except the airspace. The Class Bravo airspace should be expanded to include WIGZU the FAF for RWY 16L. This would allow us the ability to talk to VFR aircraft that constantly cross this area around 2;500 feet. We have conducted pilot outreach at surrounding airports. It has not been successful. We just want the ability to talk to these VFR aircraft to provide traffic advisories. The airspace needs to be updated.

column121: My aircraft does not have ADSB. If I depart Merrill to the north and do not receive the Part 93 deviation from ATC; then because of the combination of Part 93 and the ADSB requirement over Anchorage Class C; I am required to fly over Cook Inlet below 600 ft. MSL; which puts the aircraft in a position out of gliding distance to land. Recent local memory has multiple examples of this situation ending in a fatality. There is a large number of general aviation aircraft operating in the Anchorage area that are ADSB negative and that fatal outcome is likely to occur again in the future due to the airspace rule combinations currently in place.
